diagrams.description: This timeline traces the history of Physical Anthropology. Early classification began with Linnaeus (1735) and Blumenbach’s racial typology (1775). The 19th century saw Darwin’s 'On the Origin of Species' (1859) and Huxley’s 'Evidence as to Man's Place in Nature' (1863). The early 20th century brought Dubois’ discovery of Pithecanthropus (1891) and Dart’s Australopithecus (1925). Mid‑century witnessed the modern synthesis (Huxley, 1942), Leakey’s Olduvai finds (1959), and Washburn’s “new physical anthropology” (1951). The 1960s‑70s introduced molecular anthropology (Sarich & Wilson, 1967) and Goodall’s chimpanzee behavior research. The late 20th century refined 'Out of Africa' with mitochondrial Eve (1987). Recent decades have unlocked the Neanderthal genome (Pääbo, 2010) and Denisovan discoveries, integrating genomics and bioinformatics into the study of human evolution.

timeline title History of Physical Anthropology section 18th–19th Century 1735 : Linnaeus
Systema Naturae (classifies humans) 1775 : Blumenbach
Racial classification 1859 : Darwin
On the Origin of Species 1863 : Huxley
Man's Place in Nature section Early 20th Century 1891 : Dubois
Pithecanthropus erectus 1925 : Dart
Australopithecus africanus 1942 : Modern synthesis
(Huxley) section Mid‑20th Century 1951 : Washburn
The New Physical Anthropology 1959 : Mary Leakey
Zinjanthropus (Olduvai) 1960s : Goodall begins
chimpanzee field studies section Molecular Era 1967 : Sarich & Wilson
Molecular clock 1987 : Cann, Stoneking & Wilson
Mitochondrial Eve section Genomic Revolution 2010 : Pääbo
Neanderthal genome draft 2010s : Denisovan discoveries
ancient genomics 2020s : Paleoproteomics
and AI in fossil analysis
